title: Install dependencies and set up project structure
description: Install all required Python packages and create the project directory structure for reproducible experiments.

steps.step1: Install Python packages: pandas>=2.0, numpy>=1.24, scikit-learn>=1.3, scipy>=1.11, matplotlib>=3.7, seaborn>=0.12, fuzzywuzzy>=0.18, python-Levenshtein>=0.21, recordlinkage>=0.16, openml>=0.14, requests>=2.31, statsmodels>=0.14, tqdm>=4.65, networkx>=3.1
steps.step2: Create project directory structure: src/ (operators/, pipeline/, optimizer/, evaluation/, utils/), data/ (raw/, processed/, ground_truth/, profiles/), results/ (interaction_profiles/, baselines/, iapo/, ablations/, hypothesis_tests/), figures/
steps.step3: Create a config.py with global settings: RANDOM_SEEDS = [42, 123, 456], NUM_CORES = 2, MAX_PIPELINE_LENGTH = 8, QUALITY_METRICS = ['downstream_accuracy', 'downstream_f1', 'completeness', 'consistency'], SIGNIFICANCE_LEVEL = 0.05, INTERACTION_THRESHOLD = 0.01, SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD = 0.5, CONFIDENCE_CV_THRESHOLD = 1.0
steps.step4: Create a run_all.py master script that orchestrates all experiments sequentially and logs runtime for each stage

title: Download and preprocess 18 benchmark datasets
description: Obtain 18 real-world datasets with naturally occurring data quality issues from three established benchmarks: CleanML (10 datasets), REIN (4 datasets), and OpenML (4 datasets). This diverse collection ensures the interaction predictor has sufficient training data and covers varied error profiles.

steps.step1: Download 10 CleanML datasets via OpenML API or direct URL: (1) Adult/USCensus (OpenML ID 1590, ~48K rows, mixed types, ~7% missing), (2) Credit-G (OpenML ID 31, 1000 rows, mixed types), (3) EEG Eye State (OpenML ID 1471, ~15K rows, numeric-heavy), (4) Marketing/Bank (OpenML ID 1461, ~45K rows, mixed types), (5) Titanic (OpenML ID 40945, ~1300 rows, ~20% missing), (6) Movie (from CleanML GitHub, entity resolution task), (7) Company (from CleanML GitHub, entity matching), (8) Restaurant (from CleanML GitHub, deduplication task), (9) Sensor (from CleanML GitHub, numeric time-series), (10) Airbnb (from CleanML GitHub, pricing prediction with text and missing values). For large datasets (>10K rows), subsample to 5000 rows with stratified sampling (seed=42) to keep experiment runtime manageable.
steps.step2: Download 4 REIN datasets: (11) Cardiotocography (OpenML ID 1466, ~2126 rows, healthcare), (12) German Credit (OpenML ID 31 variant or direct download, 1000 rows, finance), (13) Steel Plates Faults (OpenML ID 1504, ~1941 rows, industrial), (14) Anneal (OpenML ID 2, ~898 rows, manufacturing with ~60% missing in some columns). Source from REIN GitHub repository or OpenML.
steps.step3: Download 4 OpenML datasets with diverse error profiles: (15) Hepatitis (OpenML ID 55, 155 rows, ~48% missing rate), (16) Labor (OpenML ID 4, 57 rows, small with missing values), (17) Soybean (OpenML ID 42, 683 rows, multi-class with inconsistencies), (18) Vote (OpenML ID 56, 435 rows, political survey with systematic missing values). These provide edge cases: very small datasets, very high missing rates, and domain diversity.
steps.step4: For each dataset, compute and save lightweight dataset characteristic features (O(n) scan): (a) missing_rate = fraction of missing cells, (b) outlier_rate = fraction of numeric cells flagged by IQR method, (c) duplicate_rate = fraction of exact duplicate rows, (d) num_numeric_cols and num_categorical_cols, (e) dataset_size = rows * cols, (f) class_imbalance_ratio for classification targets, (g) numeric_skewness_mean = mean absolute skewness of numeric columns, (h) cardinality_ratio = mean(unique values / total values) for categorical columns. Save as data/profiles/dataset_features.json with one entry per dataset.
steps.step5: For each dataset, identify the target column for downstream ML evaluation. Split into train (70%) and test (30%) using stratified splitting with seed=42. Save splits as data/processed/{dataset_name}_train.csv and data/processed/{dataset_name}_test.csv. IMPORTANT: all cleaning operators are fit only on training data to prevent data leakage.
steps.step6: For 3 datasets with ground truth available (where we inject controlled errors): select datasets with low natural error rates, inject 10% MCAR missing values, 5% outliers (multiply random numeric cells by 10), and 3% near-duplicate rows. Save clean originals as data/ground_truth/ for intrinsic quality measurement.
steps.step7: Generate a comprehensive summary statistics table for all 18 datasets: name, source (CleanML/REIN/OpenML), rows, columns, missing rate, outlier rate, duplicate rate, number of numeric/categorical features, target type, domain. Save as data/dataset_summary.csv

title: Implement 8 data cleaning operator categories
description: Implement all 8 data cleaning operator categories from the proposal, each with a consistent interface (fit on training data, transform any data). Each operator takes a DataFrame and returns a cleaned DataFrame. This covers the full scope promised: imputation, outlier removal, deduplication, format standardization, type coercion, constraint repair, normalization, and categorical encoding.

steps.step1: Define base class CleaningOperator with methods: fit(df_train) -> self, transform(df) -> df_cleaned, fit_transform(df_train) -> df_cleaned. All operators must be stateful (fit learns parameters from training data only) and deterministic given a random seed.
steps.step2: Implement operator 1 — MissingValueImputer: For numeric columns, impute with median (robust to outliers). For categorical columns, impute with mode. Uses sklearn SimpleImputer internally. Track which columns had missing values and their rates.
steps.step3: Implement operator 2 — OutlierRemover: Detect outliers using IQR method (1.5*IQR beyond Q1/Q3) on numeric columns. Replace outlier values with the nearest boundary value (winsorization) rather than dropping rows to preserve dataset size. Record number of outliers found per column.
steps.step4: Implement operator 3 — DuplicateRemover: Detect and remove exact duplicate rows (keeping first occurrence). For datasets with string columns, also check for near-duplicates using Jaccard similarity with threshold=0.9 on concatenated string columns. Record number of duplicates removed.
steps.step5: Implement operator 4 — FormatStandardizer: Standardize string formats: strip whitespace, normalize case (lowercase), remove special characters from numeric-looking strings, standardize date formats to ISO 8601, normalize boolean representations ('yes'/'no', 'Y'/'N', 'true'/'false', '1'/'0' -> consistent format).
steps.step6: Implement operator 5 — TypeCoercer: Attempt to cast string columns containing numeric-looking values to numeric types (using pd.to_numeric with errors='coerce'). Convert date-like strings to datetime. Infer and fix inconsistent column types. Track number of successful type conversions.
steps.step7: Implement operator 6 — ConstraintRepair: Implement simple functional dependency (FD) based repair. For each dataset, automatically discover approximate FDs from training data using a lightweight algorithm (check column pairs where one determines the other with >95% confidence). Repair violations by replacing the dependent value with the most frequent value for the determinant. This is a simplified but genuine constraint repair operator.
steps.step8: Implement operator 7 — ValueNormalizer: Apply z-score normalization (standardization) to all numeric columns. Fit mean and std on training data only. Handle zero-variance columns by leaving them unchanged. This operator is typically applied late in the pipeline before ML model training.
steps.step9: Implement operator 8 — CategoricalEncoder: Apply one-hot encoding for categorical columns with <=10 unique values, ordinal encoding for those with >10. Handle unknown categories at test time by mapping to a special 'unknown' category. Use sklearn's OneHotEncoder and OrdinalEncoder with handle_unknown='infrequent_if_exist' or custom handling.
steps.step10: Write unit tests for each operator: verify (a) fit_transform produces valid output (no unexpected NaN, correct types), (b) fit then transform on new data works correctly, (c) operators are deterministic with fixed seed, (d) operators handle edge cases (all-missing column, single-value column, empty DataFrame after previous operator)

title: Implement interaction measurement framework
description: Build the framework to measure main effects, pairwise interaction effects, and order sensitivity for all operator pairs on each dataset, following the formal definitions in the proposal.

steps.step1: Implement quality evaluation function evaluate_quality(df_train_cleaned, df_test_cleaned, y_train, y_test, metric='downstream_f1') that: (a) trains a RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=10, random_state=seed) on cleaned training data, (b) predicts on cleaned test data, (c) returns F1-score (macro-averaged for multiclass). Use LogisticRegression as secondary evaluator for speed. For regression datasets, use RMSE with RandomForestRegressor. Catch and handle cases where cleaning removes all rows or all features (return quality=0).
steps.step2: Implement caching layer: since many pairwise evaluations share intermediate results, cache Q(D), Q(Oi(D)), and Oi(D) (the transformed dataset itself) for each operator-dataset-seed combination. Use a dictionary keyed by (operator_name, dataset_name, seed). This reduces redundant computation from O(n^2) operator applications to O(n) per dataset.
steps.step3: Implement main_effect(operator, dataset, seed) function: computes ME(O_i, D) = Q(O_i(D)) - Q(D) by evaluating quality before and after applying the single operator. Use cached values.
steps.step4: Implement interaction_effect(op_i, op_j, dataset, seed) function: computes IE(O_i, O_j, D) = Q(O_i(O_j(D))) - Q(O_j(D)) - ME(O_i, D). This measures how much op_i's effectiveness changes when applied after op_j versus alone.
steps.step5: Implement order_sensitivity(op_i, op_j, dataset, seed) function: computes OS(O_i, O_j, D) = |Q(O_i(O_j(D))) - Q(O_j(O_i(D)))|. This measures the asymmetry of the pair.
steps.step6: Implement classify_interaction(ie_value, os_value, threshold=0.01) that categorizes each pair as: Commutative (OS < threshold), Synergistic (IE > threshold), Antagonistic (IE < -threshold), Order-Critical (OS > 3*threshold), or Independent (both |IE| < threshold and OS < threshold).
steps.step7: Implement run_all_pairwise(operators, dataset, seeds) that: for each seed, for each ordered pair (O_i, O_j) where i != j, computes ME, IE, and OS. Returns a DataFrame with columns [op_i, op_j, seed, dataset, main_effect_i, main_effect_j, interaction_effect, order_sensitivity, category]. Save results to results/interaction_profiles/{dataset_name}_interactions.csv
steps.step8: Add statistical significance testing: for each operator pair, across all datasets and seeds, perform a one-sample t-test on IE values (H0: IE=0) and on OS values (H0: OS=0). Record p-values. Apply Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons (56 pairs). Flag pairs with corrected p < 0.05 as statistically significant. Use scipy.stats.ttest_1samp.

title: Run interaction characterization across all 18 datasets
description: Execute the pairwise interaction measurement across all 18 datasets with 3 random seeds each. This is the core empirical contribution. Estimated runtime: with caching, ~56 pairwise evals per dataset per seed, each ~8 sec -> 56 * 18 * 3 * 8 / 3600 ~ 2.2 hours.

steps.step1: Run run_all_pairwise for each of the 18 datasets with seeds [42, 123, 456]. Process datasets sequentially (to manage memory on 128GB RAM). Log progress with tqdm showing dataset x seed completion. Save per-dataset results to results/interaction_profiles/{dataset_name}_interactions.csv. Log wall-clock time for each dataset.
steps.step2: Aggregate results across all datasets into a single DataFrame. Compute mean and std of IE and OS for each operator pair across datasets and seeds. Save as results/interaction_profiles/aggregated_interactions.csv
steps.step3: Compute interaction summary statistics: (a) percentage of operator pairs with statistically significant IE (Bonferroni-corrected p<0.05), (b) percentage of pairs that are order-sensitive (OS > 0.01), (c) distribution of interaction categories across all dataset-pair combinations, (d) top-5 most synergistic and top-5 most antagonistic pairs, (e) pairs with highest order sensitivity. Save as results/interaction_profiles/interaction_summary.json
steps.step4: Correlate interaction effects with dataset characteristics: for each of the 56 operator pairs, compute Spearman rank correlation between IE values (across 18 datasets) and each of the 8 dataset characteristic features. Identify which features are most predictive of strong interactions. Save correlation matrix as results/interaction_profiles/feature_interaction_correlations.csv
steps.step5: Derive rule-based heuristics from the empirical results: for each operator pair, if the sign of the interaction effect is consistent across >80% of datasets where the relevant data quality issue is present (e.g., missing_rate > 0.1 for imputation-related pairs), formulate a rule. Example rules: 'if missing_rate > 0.2, imputation before outlier detection is antagonistic', 'if duplicate_rate > 0.05, deduplication before constraint repair is synergistic'. Save rules as results/interaction_profiles/interaction_rules.json with fields: operator_pair, condition, predicted_sign, confidence, supporting_datasets.

title: Build and evaluate Interaction-Aware Pipeline Optimizer (IAPO)
description: Implement IAPO with the two-tier prediction strategy (rule-based heuristics + dataset-similarity-weighted lookup) and fallback mechanism. Uses leave-one-dataset-out cross-validation to evaluate generalization.

steps.step1: Implement Tier 1 — Rule-based interaction predictor: load the rules derived in the previous step. For a new dataset, evaluate each rule's condition against the dataset's characteristic features. If a rule fires, predict the interaction sign and approximate magnitude (use the mean |IE| from datasets where the rule was derived). Rules are interpretable and require no training data.
steps.step2: Implement Tier 2 — Dataset-similarity-weighted lookup: for operator pairs not covered by rules (or where no rule fires), compute cosine similarity between the new dataset's feature vector (8 features, z-score normalized) and each training dataset's feature vector. Return the similarity-weighted average of observed interaction effects: IE_pred = sum(sim_i * IE_i) / sum(sim_i) where sim_i = max(0, cosine_similarity). This avoids the underdetermined regression problem that Ridge regression would face with few datasets.
steps.step3: Implement prediction confidence estimation: confidence is LOW if (a) no training dataset has cosine similarity > 0.5 to the input dataset, OR (b) the similarity-weighted predictions have high variance (coefficient of variation > 1.0 across the weighted predictions). Return confidence flag alongside each prediction.
steps.step4: Implement build_interaction_graph(predicted_interactions) using networkx: create a directed weighted graph where nodes are operators and edge weight from O_i to O_j = predicted IE(O_j applied after O_i). Positive weight = synergistic ordering, negative = antagonistic.
steps.step5: Implement generate_candidate_pipelines(interaction_graph, K=10): (a) find the ordering that maximizes total synergy by solving a weighted Hamiltonian path approximation (greedy: start with highest synergy edge, extend), (b) generate K-1 variants by local perturbations (swap adjacent operators with weak edge weight), (c) return K candidate pipelines.
steps.step6: Implement fallback strategy: when prediction confidence is low, IAPO automatically (a) increases K to min(K*2, 20), and (b) if still low confidence, falls back to greedy beam search: start with empty pipeline, at each step try appending each remaining operator, keep top-2 beams. This ensures robust performance for out-of-distribution datasets.
steps.step7: Implement iapo_optimize(dataset, dataset_features, training_data, K=10, seed) that: profile dataset -> predict interactions via Tier1/Tier2 -> estimate confidence -> build graph -> generate candidates (with fallback if needed) -> evaluate all candidates -> return best pipeline, quality, num_evaluations, wall-clock time.
steps.step8: Run IAPO on all 18 datasets with 3 seeds using leave-one-dataset-out: for each held-out dataset, train rules and similarity lookup on the remaining 17 datasets, then optimize the held-out dataset. Record: best pipeline found, quality score, number of pipelines evaluated, whether fallback was triggered, wall-clock time. Save results to results/iapo/iapo_results.csv

title: Implement and run baseline pipeline construction methods
description: Implement 4 baseline methods for pipeline construction to compare against IAPO. All baselines use the same 8 operators and evaluation function. Includes one simple baseline (random), one strong baseline (greedy), one oracle (exhaustive on 5-operator subsets + large random sample for full 8), and one fixed-order baseline.

steps.step1: Baseline 1 — Random Search: Randomly sample 100 permutations of the 8 operators (out of 8!=40320 total), evaluate each, return the best. This is the simple baseline. Run on all 18 datasets x 3 seeds. Save results to results/baselines/random_search.csv. Record best quality, mean quality of sampled pipelines, and number of evaluations (100).
steps.step2: Baseline 2 — Greedy Forward Selection: Start with empty pipeline. At each step, try appending each remaining operator and keep the one that gives the best quality improvement. This requires sum(8,7,...,1) = 36 evaluations. This is a strong heuristic baseline. Run on all 18 datasets x 3 seeds. Save results to results/baselines/greedy.csv
steps.step3: Baseline 3 — Approximate Exhaustive Search (Oracle): Since 8! = 40320 is too expensive for all datasets, use a two-pronged approach: (a) For 5 representative datasets (selected to span diverse characteristics: one high-missing, one high-outlier, one high-duplicate, one large, one small), run true exhaustive search over all permutations of the 5 most impactful operators (5!=120 evaluations). (b) For all 18 datasets, sample 500 random permutations of all 8 operators and take the best as an approximate upper bound. Save results to results/baselines/exhaustive.csv. Estimated runtime: 120 * 5 * 3 * 8 + 500 * 18 * 3 * 8 sec ~ 62 hours... TOO SLOW. Instead: use faster evaluation (LogisticRegression, ~1 sec per eval). Revised: 120*5*3*1 + 500*18*3*1 = 1800 + 27000 = 28800 sec ~ 8 hours. Still tight. Reduce to: exhaustive on 5 operators for 5 datasets (1800 evals * 1 sec = 0.5 hr) + 200 random for all 18 datasets (200*18*3*1 = 10800 sec = 3 hr). Total ~3.5 hours. Use LogisticRegression for exhaustive/random baselines to save time, then evaluate the winning pipeline with RandomForest for final quality.
steps.step4: Baseline 4 — Fixed Canonical Order: Use a single fixed ordering based on common textbook recommendations: TypeCoercion -> FormatStandardization -> DuplicateRemoval -> MissingValueImputation -> OutlierRemoval -> ConstraintRepair -> ValueNormalization -> CategoricalEncoding. No search required. Run on all 18 datasets x 3 seeds. Save results to results/baselines/canonical.csv
steps.step5: Compile baseline comparison table: for each method, record (a) mean quality (F1) across datasets and seeds, (b) quality as percentage of approximate exhaustive optimum, (c) number of pipeline evaluations required, (d) wall-clock time. Save as results/baselines/baseline_comparison.csv

title: Ablation studies for IAPO components
description: Conduct ablation studies to validate each novel component of IAPO and understand what drives performance. Each ablation isolates one design decision.

steps.step1: Ablation 1 — No interaction modeling (main effects only): Replace IAPO's interaction predictions with a simple strategy that ranks operators by individual main effect magnitude and orders them accordingly (best individual operator first). This tests whether interaction modeling adds value beyond knowing which operators are individually most effective. Run on all 18 datasets x 3 seeds. Save to results/ablations/main_effects_only.csv
steps.step2: Ablation 2 — Tier 1 only (rules only, no similarity lookup): Use only the rule-based heuristics without the dataset-similarity-weighted lookup for interaction prediction. For operator pairs where no rule fires, assume IE=0 (independent). This isolates the contribution of the similarity-based component. Run on all 18 datasets x 3 seeds. Save to results/ablations/rules_only.csv
steps.step3: Ablation 3 — Tier 2 only (similarity lookup only, no rules): Use only the dataset-similarity-weighted lookup without rule-based heuristics. This isolates the contribution of the rule-based component. Run on all 18 datasets x 3 seeds. Save to results/ablations/similarity_only.csv
steps.step4: Ablation 4 — Number of candidates K: Run full IAPO with K = 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 candidates on all 18 datasets x 3 seeds. Plot quality vs. K to show diminishing returns and identify the sweet spot. Save to results/ablations/vary_K.csv
steps.step5: Ablation 5 — Without fallback strategy: Run IAPO without the fallback mechanism (always use K=10, never increase K or fall back to greedy beam search). Compare against full IAPO to measure the value of the fallback, especially on datasets where confidence is low. Save to results/ablations/no_fallback.csv
steps.step6: Ablation 6 — Operator subset scaling: Run both IAPO and Random Search with subsets of 4, 5, 6, 7, and all 8 operators. Compare the ratio IAPO_quality / RandomSearch_quality as the number of operators grows. IAPO's advantage should increase as the search space grows factorially. Save to results/ablations/operator_scaling.csv
steps.step7: Ablation 7 — Feature importance for interaction prediction: Train the similarity-weighted lookup with subsets of dataset features: (a) only missing_rate and outlier_rate (2 features), (b) all data quality features (missing_rate, outlier_rate, duplicate_rate — 3 features), (c) all 8 features. Compare leave-one-out prediction accuracy with each feature set. Save to results/ablations/feature_importance.csv

title: Statistical analysis and hypothesis testing
description: Formally test the three components of the hypothesis using statistical tests and compute all success criteria metrics.

steps.step1: Hypothesis 1 — Systematicity of interactions: For each of the 56 ordered operator pairs, compute the mean IE across all 18 datasets x 3 seeds. Run a one-sample t-test (H0: IE=0) with Bonferroni correction. Count the percentage of pairs with significant non-zero IE. Success criterion: >= 50% of pairs show p < 0.05 after correction. Also compute the percentage with consistent sign (synergistic or antagonistic) across >75% of datasets.
steps.step2: Hypothesis 2 — Predictability: Evaluate the two-tier predictor using leave-one-dataset-out cross-validation. For rule-based heuristics: compute accuracy of sign prediction (does the rule correctly predict synergistic vs. antagonistic?) across all held-out datasets. Success criterion: >= 70% sign accuracy. For similarity-weighted lookup: compute Spearman rank correlation between predicted and actual IE magnitudes. Success criterion: rho > 0.5. Report both metrics separately and for the combined two-tier system.
steps.step3: Hypothesis 3 — Pipeline optimization efficiency: For each of the 18 datasets (averaged over 3 seeds), compute IAPO quality as percentage of approximate exhaustive search optimum. Success criterion: >= 95% quality with <= 10% search cost (num_evaluations / total_possible_evaluations). Compare IAPO against each baseline using Wilcoxon signed-rank test (paired, non-parametric, appropriate for 18 paired observations) across datasets.
steps.step4: Compute effect sizes: Cohen's d for IAPO vs. each baseline. Report 95% confidence intervals for mean quality differences using bootstrap (10000 resamples).
steps.step5: Create a comprehensive results summary with all success criteria, measured values, statistical test results, and pass/fail determination. Save as results/hypothesis_tests/hypothesis_results.json and results/hypothesis_tests/hypothesis_results.csv

title: Generate paper figures and tables
description: Create all figures and tables needed for the research paper. Set consistent style: matplotlib rcParams with font.size=12, figure.dpi=300, PDF output, tight_layout.

steps.step1: Figure 1 — Interaction Effect Heatmap: 8x8 heatmap showing mean IE for each ordered operator pair (O_i applied after O_j), averaged across all 18 datasets. Diverging colormap (blue=antagonistic, white=independent, red=synergistic). Annotate cells with mean IE value. Mark statistically significant pairs with * (Bonferroni-corrected p<0.05). Diagonal is N/A. Save as figures/fig1_interaction_heatmap.pdf
steps.step2: Figure 2 — Order Sensitivity Heatmap: 8x8 symmetric heatmap showing mean OS for each unordered operator pair. Sequential colormap (white=commutative, dark=order-critical). Annotate with OS values. Save as figures/fig2_order_sensitivity_heatmap.pdf
steps.step3: Figure 3 — Interaction Category Distribution: Stacked bar chart with one bar per operator pair (or grouped by operator), showing the percentage of dataset observations in each interaction category (Commutative, Synergistic, Antagonistic, Order-Critical, Independent) across the 18 datasets. Save as figures/fig3_interaction_categories.pdf
steps.step4: Figure 4 — Method Comparison Bar Chart: Grouped bar chart comparing mean downstream F1 of IAPO, Random Search (100), Greedy, Canonical Order, and Approximate Exhaustive across all 18 datasets. Include error bars (std across seeds). Add horizontal line for exhaustive upper bound. Save as figures/fig4_method_comparison.pdf
steps.step5: Figure 5 — Search Efficiency Frontier: Scatter plot with x-axis = number of pipeline evaluations (log scale), y-axis = quality as percentage of exhaustive optimum. One point per method (with error bars across datasets). Shows IAPO's Pareto-optimal position. Save as figures/fig5_search_efficiency.pdf
steps.step6: Figure 6 — Ablation: K vs Quality: Line plot showing mean IAPO quality vs. K (1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20) with shaded error bands (std across datasets). Mark the default K=10 with a vertical dashed line. Save as figures/fig6_ablation_K.pdf
steps.step7: Figure 7 — Dataset Feature Correlation: Heatmap showing Spearman correlation between the 8 dataset characteristics and the IE values for the top-10 most order-sensitive operator pairs. Reveals which dataset properties predict strong interactions. Save as figures/fig7_feature_correlations.pdf
steps.step8: Figure 8 — Operator Scaling: Line plot showing quality ratio (IAPO / Random Search) vs. number of operators (4, 5, 6, 7, 8). IAPO's advantage should grow as the search space grows. Save as figures/fig8_operator_scaling.pdf
steps.step9: Figure 9 — Per-Dataset Results: Horizontal bar chart showing IAPO quality vs. exhaustive optimum for each of the 18 datasets individually. Datasets sorted by IAPO's relative performance. Helps identify where IAPO excels vs. struggles. Save as figures/fig9_per_dataset.pdf
steps.step10: Table 1 — Dataset Summary: Formatted table with all 18 datasets: name, source, domain, rows, columns, missing rate, outlier rate, duplicate rate, target type. Save as figures/table1_datasets.csv and .tex
steps.step11: Table 2 — Main Results: Per-dataset F1 for all methods + mean. Bold best non-oracle method per dataset. Include number of evaluations per method. Save as figures/table2_main_results.csv and .tex
steps.step12: Table 3 — Ablation Results: Summary table showing mean quality for each ablation variant vs. full IAPO. Include statistical significance markers. Save as figures/table3_ablations.csv and .tex
steps.step13: Table 4 — Hypothesis Test Summary: Three-row table (one per hypothesis component) with columns: claim, success criterion, measured value, pass/fail, p-value. Save as figures/table4_hypothesis.csv and .tex
